issues.description: Short JWT secrets can be brute-forced, allowing attackers to forge authentication tokens.
issues.recommendation: Use a secret key with at least 32–64 characters and high entropy.

issues.description: Cookies can be transmitted over HTTP and intercepted by attackers.
issues.recommendation: Enable Secure flag to ensure cookies are only sent over HTTPS.

issues.description: Long sessions increase the risk of session hijacking.
issues.recommendation: Reduce session timeout to 900–1800 seconds (15–30 minutes).

issues.description: Cookies accessible to JavaScript increase the risk of XSS-based session theft.
Absence of MFA increases risk from credential compromise.
issues.recommendation: Enable MFA for account-sensitive operations and logins.

issues.description: Storing tokens in localStorage exposes them to XSS attacks.
issues.recommendation: Store tokens in secure, HttpOnly cookies or use secure storage mechanisms.

issues.description: Without rotating refresh tokens, stolen tokens remain usable for longer.
issues.recommendation: Enable refresh token rotation and revoke on reuse.

issues.description: SameSite=None allows cross-site requests and can increase CSRF risk unless combined with other protections.
issues.recommendation: Prefer SameSite=Lax or Strict for auth cookies when possible, and pair with CSRF protections.

issues.description: MD5 and similar fast hashes are unsuitable for password storage.
issues.recommendation: Use adaptive hashing such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 with appropriate parameters.
